---
title: A few more of our favourite tools
aliases:
  - tools2.html
author:
  - Marie-Hélène Burle
  - Alex Razoumov
---

:::{.def}

[In a previous webinar](wb_tools1.qmd), we presented three of our favourite command line tools. Today, we will introduce other tools we find really useful in our daily workflow:

- [lazygit](https://github.com/jesseduffield/lazygit): a wonderful terminal UI for Git,
- [bat](https://github.com/sharkdp/bat): a great syntax highlighter,
- [ripgrep](https://github.com/BurntSushi/ripgrep): a fast alternative to `grep`,
- [fd](https://github.com/sharkdp/fd): a /really/ fast alternative to `find`,
- [pass](https://www.passwordstore.org/): a command line password manager.

Along the way, I will use a few other neat command line tools such as [hyperfine](https://github.com/sharkdp/hyperfine)—for sophisticated benchmarking—and [diff-so-fancy](https://github.com/so-fancy/diff-so-fancy)—which makes your diffs a lot more readable.

For the Emacs users among you, we will finish the workshop with two Emacs utilities:

- [TRAMP](https://www.emacswiki.org/emacs/TrampMode): a remote file access system,
- [Helm](https://github.com/emacs-helm/helm): a "framework for incremental completions and narrowing selections".

:::

{{< video https://www.youtube.com/embed/nshNKPFDrV8 >}}

<script type="application/ld+json">
{
  "@context": "https://schema.org",
  "@type": "LearningResource",
  "name": "More fun tools for the command line",
  "dateCreated": "2022-03-16",
  "description": "[In a previous webinar](https://mint.westdri.ca/tools/wb_tools1), we presented three of our favourite command line tools. Today, we will introduce other tools we find really useful in our daily workflow:\n\n- [lazygit](https://github.com/jesseduffield/lazygit): a wonderful terminal UI for Git,\n\n- [bat](https://github.com/sharkdp/bat): a great syntax highlighter,\n\n- [ripgrep](https://github.com/BurntSushi/ripgrep): a fast alternative to `grep`,\n\n- [fd](https://github.com/sharkdp/fd): a /really/ fast alternative to `find`,\n\n- [pass](https://www.passwordstore.org/): a command line password manager.\n\nAlong the way, I will use a few other neat command line tools such as [hyperfine](https://github.com/sharkdp/hyperfine)—for sophisticated benchmarking—and [diff-so-fancy](https://github.com/so-fancy/diff-so-fancy)—which makes your diffs a lot more readable.\n\nFor the Emacs users among you, we will finish the workshop with two Emacs utilities:\n\n- [TRAMP](https://www.emacswiki.org/emacs/TrampMode): a remote file access system,\n\n- [Helm](https://github.com/emacs-helm/helm): a 'framework for incremental completions and narrowing selections'.",
  "url": "https://mint.westdri.ca/tools/wb_tools2",
  "learningResourceType": "video",
  "inLanguage": "en",
  "license": "https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc-sa/4.0/",
  "provider": {
    "@type": "Organization",
    "name": "SFU Research Computing Group"
  },
  "keywords": "lazygit, bat, ripgrep, rg, fd, pass, hyperfine, diff-so-fancy, Git, cat, find, grep, TUI, CLI, command line, Bash, Zsh, Unix shell, Emacs, TRAMP",
  "author": [
    {
      "@type": "Person",
      "name": "Marie-Hélène Burle"
    },
    {
      "@type": "Person",
      "name": "Alex Razoumov"
    }
  ],
  "educationalLevel": "intermediate"
}
</script>
